does: PURGE presents a machine unlearning algorithm that exploits the duality between continual learning and unlearning by adapting gradient projection constraints from A-GEM to ensure unlearning steps do not degrade retain-set performance. The method combines output-level suppression with multi-layer representation erasure, pushing intermediate-layer activations of forget-set samples toward the retain-set distribution to remove information from hidden representations. A core innovation is replacing uniform distribution targets with the model's natural confusion pattern on retain data, making the unlearned model more resistant to membership inference attacks that would otherwise detect the erasure through suspicious uniform outputs on forgotten data.
connects: This work builds on the growing intersection of continual learning and machine unlearning literature by formalizing their theoretical duality—an insight that has motivated recent work but remains under-exploited in practice. It extends A-GEM's gradient projection approach, originally designed for catastrophic forgetting in continual learning, to the unlearning setting where the goal is intentional data erasure rather than task retention. The paper responds to limitations of prior unlearning methods that achieve high retain accuracy but remain vulnerable to membership inference attacks, connecting to security and privacy evaluation frameworks that assess unlearning quality beyond simple accuracy metrics and addressing critiques of earlier approaches that naively target uniform distributions.
enables: This work opens several future directions for practical machine unlearning deployment. The retain-confusion targeting mechanism could inspire more sophisticated defenses against inference attacks in unlearning, motivating research into provably secure unlearning guarantees. The multi-layer representation erasure approach suggests new architectures and training procedures designed specifically for efficient unlearning, potentially enabling users to control model behavior post-deployment without retraining. The duality perspective between continual learning and unlearning could unlock transfer of techniques across these communities, including more efficient memory-replay methods adapted to unlearning constraints and formal analysis of the fundamental limits of simultaneous retention and erasure.
